sourceTitle: Three key databases for teams, and how to use them

questions.question: Which three databases does Notion recommend as essential for every team?

options.text: Meeting Notes, Docs, and Tasks & Projects
options.explanation: These are the three core databases highlighted in the guide.

options.text: Contacts, Calendars, and Files
options.explanation: While useful, they are not the three key databases discussed.

options.text: Ideas, Bugs, and Roadmaps
options.explanation: These are example content types, not the three core databases.

options.text: HR, Finance, and Marketing
options.explanation: These are departments, not the database categories presented.

questions.question: Which database view is most suitable for moving tasks across status columns like “In Progress” → “Done”?

options.text: Table view
options.explanation: Tables list items but don’t provide drag‑and‑drop status columns.

options.text: Board view (Kanban)
options.explanation: Board view groups cards by a status property, enabling drag‑and‑drop between columns.

options.text: Calendar view
options.explanation: Calendars plot items by date, not by status.

options.text: List view
options.explanation: Lists are simple vertical lists without status columns.

questions.question: How do you create a new page inside a Notion database?

options.text: Press Ctrl+N anywhere on the page
options.explanation: Ctrl+N creates a new Notion page, not a database entry.

options.text: Click the “+ New” button at the bottom or top of the database
options.explanation: The guide specifies using the “+ New” button to add a fully editable page.

options.text: Drag an existing page into the database
options.explanation: Dragging moves a page but does not create a new entry.

options.text: Select “Create Template” from the sidebar
options.explanation: Templates are for reusing structure, not for a one‑off new page.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using a template in the Meeting Notes database for recurring meetings?

options.text: It automatically sends meeting invites to attendees
options.explanation: Templates don’t handle calendar invites.

options.text: It pre‑populates standard properties and page content, speeding up note‑taking and ensuring consistency
options.explanation: Templates include pre‑filled properties and sections, which is the benefit described.

options.text: It locks the page so no one can edit after the meeting
options.explanation: Templates create editable pages; they don’t lock content.

options.text: It creates a separate database for each meeting type
options.explanation: Templates stay within the same database.

questions.question: If you want to see only the tasks assigned to you that are due this week, which combination of features should you use?

options.text: Sort by due date and group by assignee
options.explanation: Sorting alone won’t hide other users’ tasks.

options.text: Apply two filters: Person = you, and Due date within the next 7 days
options.explanation: Filtering by both person and date restricts the view to exactly those tasks.

options.text: Create a new board view grouped by status
options.explanation: Board view groups but doesn’t filter out other users’ tasks.

options.text: Use a timeline view and manually scroll to your tasks
options.explanation: Timeline view visualizes dates but doesn’t automatically filter by assignee.

questions.question: Which property type lets you connect individual tasks to a larger project in Notion?

options.text: Select
options.explanation: Select lets you choose from predefined tags but doesn’t create a link between databases.

options.text: Relation
options.explanation: Relation properties create a link between entries in two databases, allowing tasks to be attached to projects.

options.text: Formula
options.explanation: Formulas calculate values; they don’t establish cross‑database links.

options.text: Rollup
options.explanation: Rollups pull data from a relation but require the relation to exist first.

questions.question: How can you display a visual progress bar that reflects the percentage of completed tasks within a project?

options.text: Add a Number property and manually type the percentage
options.explanation: Manual entry defeats the purpose of an automated progress bar.

options.text: Create a Rollup property on the Projects database that calculates “percent per group” of tasks with status = Complete
options.explanation: The guide explains using a rollup with “percent per group” to generate a progress bar.

options.text: Use a Formula that divides the total number of tasks by the number of completed tasks
options.explanation: The formula would be inverted; the rollup method is the recommended approach.

options.text: Apply a Filter that only shows completed tasks
options.explanation: Filtering hides incomplete tasks; it doesn’t display a progress metric.

questions.question: Which view would you choose to visualize how multiple projects overlap on the calendar and to assess workload distribution over weeks?

options.text: Board view
options.explanation: Board view groups by status, not by time.

options.text: Calendar view
options.explanation: Calendar shows dates but doesn’t illustrate duration or overlap between projects.

options.text: Timeline view
options.explanation: Timeline view displays start and end dates, letting you see overlapping projects and workload.

options.text: List view
options.explanation: List view is a simple vertical list without temporal visualization.

questions.question: How can linked database views be used to build a project dashboard that shows tasks, documents, and meeting notes without duplicating the original data?

options.text: Copy each database into the dashboard page and manually sync them daily
options.explanation: Copying creates separate copies, losing the dynamic sync benefit.

options.text: Create a single linked view that merges all three databases into one block
options.explanation: A single linked view can only reference one source database at a time.

options.text: Add three separate linked‑view blocks, each pointing to the original Tasks, Docs, and Meeting Notes databases, then apply appropriate filters and layouts for each block
options.explanation: Linked views pull live data from the source databases, allowing you to display each type in its own filtered view on the same page.

options.text: Use a Formula property to concatenate the contents of the three databases into one page
options.explanation: Formulas cannot pull full page content from other databases.

questions.question: Describe the steps to create a new database from scratch using Notion’s AI assistance, and explain why you might later adjust the pre‑selected properties.

options.text: Open a new page, click “Database”, select “Start from scratch”, then manually add each property one by one; you would never need to adjust later because you designed it perfectly.
options.explanation: This bypasses the AI option and assumes the initial setup is final, which is unrealistic.

options.text: Open a new page, click “Database”, choose “Build with AI”, describe the purpose (e.g., project tracker), let AI suggest a set of properties, accept the suggestion, and later tweak properties to match evolving team needs or to add custom tags.
options.explanation: The guide mentions building with AI, which proposes relevant properties that can be refined later as workflows change.

options.text: Select an existing template, rename it, and never change the properties because templates are immutable.
options.explanation: Templates can be edited; the statement that they are immutable is false.

options.text: Use the “Import CSV” feature to create a database, then rely on AI to automatically adjust properties as data changes.
options.explanation: Importing CSV is a method, but AI does not continuously adjust properties automatically.
